title: German Pronunciation& Sounds
description: This module comes before grammar, before vocabulary, before everything. Here is why: if you cannot recognise how German sounds, listening exercises will feel impossible no matter how much vocabulary you know.

exercises.question: How is the German letter W pronounced?
exercises.hint: Think of a German word: Wasser (water)
exercises.explanation: German W always sounds like English V. So 'Wasser' (water) is pronounced 'VAH-ser', not 'WAH-ser'. This is one of the most important differences to retrain your ear on.
exercises.rule_hint: German W → sounds like English V, always.

exercises.question: How do you pronounce the German Z?
exercises.hint: Think: Zeit (time), zwei (two)
exercises.explanation: German Z always makes the TS sound — like the end of 'bits' or 'cats'. So 'Zeit' sounds like 'TSAIT' and 'zwei' sounds like 'TSVAI'. English speakers often say 'ZAIT' — that is wrong.
exercises.rule_hint: German Z → TS sound. Zeit = TSAIT. zwei = TSVAI.

exercises.question: The German word 'Vater' (father) — how does the V sound?
exercises.hint: German V is the opposite of what you expect
exercises.explanation: German V sounds like English F. So 'Vater' is pronounced 'FAH-ter' not 'VAH-ter'. In German, the letter that sounds like English V is actually written as W.
exercises.rule_hint: German V → F sound. German W → V sound. They swap!

exercises.question: How do you produce the German ö sound?
exercises.hint: Think about your lip position
exercises.explanation: The ö is made by positioning your tongue as if saying English E (as in 'see') and then rounding your lips forward. It is the lip rounding that creates the ö — without it you just get a plain E sound.
exercises.rule_hint: ö = E tongue position + rounded lips. Practice: schön, hören, möchten.

exercises.question: Which of these is a minimal pair where vowel length changes the meaning?
exercises.hint: Look for the pair where only vowel length differs
exercises.explanation: Bett (bed) has a short E sound. Beet (flower bed) has a long E sound. Same letters, same consonants — only the vowel length changes, and the meaning is completely different. This is why vowel length matters.
exercises.rule_hint: Short vowel before double consonant (Bett). Long vowel before single consonant (Beet).

exercises.question: How is SP pronounced at the START of a German word?
exercises.hint: Think: sprechen, Spaß, Sport
exercises.explanation: At the start of a German word, SP is pronounced SHP. So 'sprechen' (to speak) sounds like 'SHPrechen', not 'SPrechen'. This rule also applies to ST at the start of a word → SHT.
exercises.rule_hint: SP at start of word → SHP. ST at start of word → SHT.

exercises.question: The word 'ich' (I) — which CH sound does it use?
exercises.hint: Look at what comes before CH: the letter I
exercises.explanation: CH after I, E, Ä, Ö, Ü makes the SOFT CH — produced at the front of the mouth, almost like a hissed H. 'Ich' uses the soft CH because it follows I. This is the most important CH sound to master since you say 'ich' constantly.
exercises.rule_hint: CH after I, E, ä, ö, ü → soft CH (front of mouth). After A, O, U → hard CH (back of throat).

exercises.question: In compound nouns like 'Krankenhaus', which syllable is stressed?
exercises.hint: German compound noun stress rule
exercises.explanation: In German compound nouns, stress always falls on the FIRST element — KRANKenhaus, not krankenHAUS. This applies to all compound nouns: BAHNhof, HAUSaufgabe, ARZTpraxis. The first element carries the meaning anchor.
exercises.rule_hint: Compound nouns: ALWAYS stress the first element.

exercises.question: In natural German speech, which words are spoken most clearly and strongly?
exercises.hint: Think about what carries meaning vs what is grammatical glue
exercises.explanation: German is stress-timed. Content words — nouns, main verbs, adjectives — carry stress and meaning and are spoken clearly. Function words — articles (der, die), prepositions (in, auf), conjunctions (und, aber) — are reduced and spoken quietly. This rhythm is what makes German sound natural.
exercises.rule_hint: Content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) = stressed. Function words (articles, prepositions) = reduced.

title: Numbers, Time& Dates
description: Numbers appear in every section of the Goethe exam. This module makes them automatic — so they never cost you marks.

lessons.title: Numbers 1 to 20 — learn these cold
lessons.content: Every compound German number is built from these 20 base numbers. They must be automatic before anything else. 🔊 Tap any card to hear it spoken aloud in German by your browser's German voice. Numbers never appear alone in the exam. They appear inside sentences. Press 🔊 to hear each sentence spoken in German.

exercises.question: What is the German word for 17?
exercises.hint: Sieben shortens before -zehn
exercises.explanation: 17 = siebzehn. Sieben loses its -en before -zehn. Same for 16: sechs → sechzehn. These two are the only shortened forms.
exercises.rule_hint: 17 = siebzehn · 16 = sechzehn — both are shortened.

exercises.question: How do Germans say zero?
exercises.hint: You hear this in every phone number
exercises.explanation: Zero = null. You will hear it constantly in phone numbers, postcodes, and addresses in Goethe Hören.
exercises.rule_hint: 0 = null. Read digit by digit in phone numbers.

exercises.question: "Das kostet zwölf Euro." How much?
exercises.hint: zwölf does not follow the teen pattern
exercises.explanation: zwölf = 12. It must be memorised — it does not follow the -zehn pattern. elf (11) and zwölf (12) are both irregular standalone words.
exercises.rule_hint: 11 = elf · 12 = zwölf — both must be memorised.

lessons.title: Numbers 20 to 1000 — the backwards pattern
lessons.content: English says "twenty-three" — tens first. German says "dreiundzwanzig" — literally three-and-twenty. Units come first, then "und", then tens. All as one word. Prices are the most common compound number context in Goethe. Press 🔊 to hear correct pronunciation.

exercises.question: How do you say 37 in German?
exercises.hint: Units FIRST, then und, then tens
exercises.explanation: 37 = siebenunddreißig. Units (sieben=7) come first, then und, then tens (dreißig=30). All one word — opposite of English.
exercises.rule_hint: German: [units]+und+[tens]. One word, no spaces.

exercises.question: "zweihundertfünfzig" in digits?
exercises.hint: Break it: zwei + hundert + fünfzig
exercises.explanation: zweihundertfünfzig = 250. Zwei(2)+hundert(100)=200, then fünfzig=50. So 200+50=250.
exercises.rule_hint: [digit]+hundert+[rest]. All stacked into one word.

lessons.title: The trap that costs the most marks in Hören
lessons.content: The examiners use the teen/tens confusion deliberately. Tap each pair and listen for the difference. The key is the ending: -zehn has a final N, -zig does not.

exercises.question: "Der Zug fährt von Gleis achtzehn ab." Which platform?
exercises.hint: Listen for the ending — N or no N?
exercises.explanation: achtzehn = 18. The -zehn ending (-TSAYN) has a clear N. achtzig (80) ends in -TSIG with no N. Platform 18 is realistic.
exercises.rule_hint: -zehn (has N) = teen · -zig (no N) = tens.

exercises.question: "Das kostet fünfzig Euro." How much?
exercises.hint: fünfzig vs fünfzehn — check the ending
exercises.explanation: fünfzig = 50. The -zig ending = tens. fünfzehn would be 15 with -zehn ending. fünfzig ends in -TSIG — no N — so 50.
exercises.rule_hint: fünfzig=50 (-zig) · fünfzehn=15 (-zehn).

lessons.title: Telling the time — two systems, both tested
lessons.content: Formal (announcements, timetables) uses the 24-hour clock. Informal (conversation) uses halb, Viertel nach, Viertel vor. Both appear in Goethe — tap any card to hear it.

exercises.question: "Der Zug fährt um halb neun ab." When?
exercises.hint: halb = half TO the next hour
exercises.explanation: halb neun = 8:30. German halb means half TO the next hour. Halfway to nine = 8:30. This is the most tested informal time in Goethe A1.
exercises.rule_hint: halb [hour] = 30 min BEFORE that hour. halb neun = 8:30.

exercises.question: How do you say 14:15 in formal German?
exercises.hint: Formal: 24-hour, hour Uhr minutes
exercises.explanation: 14:15 formal = vierzehn Uhr fünfzehn. Formal time: [24hr hour] Uhr [minutes]. 14=vierzehn, 15=fünfzehn.
exercises.rule_hint: Formal: [24hr hour] Uhr [minutes].

lessons.title: Days, months and seasons — all masculine
lessons.content: All days of the week, all months, and all seasons are masculine (der) in German. One rule covers all three categories — no exceptions.

exercises.question: What gender are ALL days, months, and seasons in German?
exercises.hint: One rule covers all three categories
exercises.explanation: All days (der Montag), months (der Januar), and seasons (der Frühling, der Sommer, der Herbst, der Winter) are masculine — der. One rule, no exceptions.
exercises.rule_hint: Days + months + seasons = ALL der. No exceptions.

exercises.question: How do you say "on Monday" in German?
exercises.hint: Days use a specific preposition
exercises.explanation: Days use "am" — am Montag (on Monday). Am = an+dem contracted. Months and seasons use "im" — im Januar, im Sommer.
exercises.rule_hint: Days → am [day]. Months/seasons → im [month/season].

lessons.title: Ordinal numbers — first, second, third
lessons.content: Ordinal numbers are used for dates, floors, and rankings. Three are irregular — memorise only those. From 4th onwards the pattern is completely regular.

exercises.question: How do you say "the 1st of May" in German?
exercises.hint: 1st is irregular — not einst-
exercises.explanation: 1st = erst- (irregular). "The 1st of May" = der erste Mai. The three irregulars: erst- (1st), zweit- (2nd), dritt- (3rd). From 4th: base+t.
exercises.rule_hint: 1st=erst- · 2nd=zweit- · 3rd=dritt- (irregular). 4th+=base+t.

exercises.question: Born 8 June 1998. What do you write on a German form?
exercises.hint: German date format: day.month.year
exercises.explanation: German format is day.month.year — so 8 June 1998 = 08.06.1998. Opposite of American format. Wrong order loses marks in Goethe Schreiben.
exercises.rule_hint: German dates: day.month.year — 08.06.1998.

exercises.question: What is 47 in German?
exercises.hint: Units first, then und, then tens
exercises.explanation: 47 = siebenundvierzig. sieben(7)+und+vierzig(40). Do not confuse with 74 = vierundsiebzig (vier=4+und+siebzig=70).
exercises.rule_hint: 47 = sieben+und+vierzig. 74 = vier+und+siebzig.

title: Greetings, Introductions& Social Scripts
description: This module is not just vocabulary. These are the exact scripts the Goethe examiner expects in the Sprechen section. Language and social intelligence together.

lessons.title: Sie vs du — the most important social rule in German
lessons.content: German has two words for "you". Sie is formal — used with strangers, professionals, older people, and the Goethe examiner. du is informal — used with friends, family, children, and peers you know well. In English, "you" covers everyone. In German, choosing the wrong one is a social mistake — and in the Goethe exam, the wrong register costs marks. Read each situation and decide: would you use Sie or du? Then press 🔊 to hear the correct German phrase.

exercises.question: You meet the Goethe examiner. Which form do you use?
exercises.hint: Think about the professional formal context
exercises.explanation: Always Sie with the Goethe examiner, without exception. The examiner is a professional in a formal testing context. Using du signals you do not understand German social norms, which costs marks in the Sprechen assessment.
exercises.rule_hint: Goethe examiner = always Sie. Never du in the exam.

exercises.question: "Wie heißen Sie?" — what does this ask?
exercises.hint: Sie = formal. heißen = to be called
exercises.explanation: "Wie heißen Sie?" = "What is your name?" (formal). heißen means "to be called." The formal version uses Sie. The informal version is "Wie heißt du?" You will hear this as the very first question in the Goethe Sprechen section.
exercises.rule_hint: Wie heißen Sie? = What is your name? (formal). Answer: Ich heiße [name].

exercises.question: A fellow student says "Wie heißt du?" and tells you their first name. What register do they use?
exercises.hint: They said "du" — what does that tell you?
exercises.explanation: "Wie heißt du?" uses du — the informal form. When someone addresses you with du and offers only their first name, they are inviting an informal relationship. You respond in kind with du and your first name.
exercises.rule_hint: du-form = informal. Respond to du with du. Respond to Sie with Sie.

lessons.title: Greetings — the right one for the right moment
lessons.content: German greetings are time-sensitive and region-sensitive. Using the wrong greeting at the wrong time of day does not cause confusion — but it signals that you are not yet comfortable with the language. The Goethe examiner notices comfort.

exercises.question: It is 8am. Which greeting is most appropriate with the Goethe examiner?
exercises.hint: Time of day matters. What is the morning greeting?
exercises.explanation: "Guten Morgen" is the correct formal greeting in the morning (until approximately midday). "Hallo" is too informal for the examiner. "Guten Abend" is evening. "Tschüss" is a goodbye — never an opening.
exercises.rule_hint: Morning (until ~midday) = Guten Morgen. Day = Guten Tag. Evening = Guten Abend.

exercises.question: The examiner asks "Wie geht es Ihnen?" What is the correct response?
exercises.hint: Answer and return the question — this is the German way
exercises.explanation: "Danke, gut. Und Ihnen?" — you say you are well, thank the person, and return the question. In German social culture, NOT returning the question is considered slightly rude. The examiner specifically listens for this social competence.
exercises.rule_hint: Always return "Wie geht es Ihnen?" with "Und Ihnen?" (formal) or "Und dir?" (informal).

lessons.title: Introducing yourself — the Goethe Sprechen script
lessons.content: The first task in the Goethe A1 Sprechen section is a self-introduction. The examiner gives you a prompt card and expects you to speak for about 60–90 seconds covering specific topics. This lesson teaches you the exact structure and language. This is a complete model self-introduction. Read it through, press 🔊 to hear each line, then close your eyes and try to say it from memory. This is exactly what the examiner wants to hear.

exercises.question: How do you say "I am a nurse" in German? Choose the correct form.
exercises.hint: Professions in German never take an article after "Ich bin"
exercises.explanation: "Ich bin Krankenschwester." — no article. In German, professions after "sein" (to be) never take an article. "Ich bin eine Krankenschwester" is incorrect. This is one of the most common mistakes in the Goethe Sprechen section.
exercises.rule_hint: Professions: Ich bin [job]. NEVER "Ich bin eine/ein [job]."

exercises.question: "Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland _____ möchte." Fill in the blank.
exercises.hint: weil sends the verb to the END of the clause
exercises.explanation: "arbeiten möchte" — correct. "weil" (because) is a subordinating conjunction that sends the verb to the end of its clause. So the modal verb "möchte" goes to the very end, after the infinitive "arbeiten." This word order rule is tested in both Schreiben and Sprechen.
exercises.rule_hint: weil + [subject] + [rest] + [verb at END]. Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich arbeiten möchte.

exercises.question: What are the 6 topics the Goethe examiner expects in your self-introduction?
exercises.hint: Think about what someone needs to know about you
options: Name, age, height, weight, colour, city
Name, origin, home, profession, family, why German
Name, number, address, email, phone, school
Greeting, name, job, salary, address, goodbye
exercises.correct_answer: Name, origin, home, profession, family, why German
exercises.explanation: The 6 expected topics: Name (Ich heiße...), Herkunft/origin (Ich komme aus...), Wohnort/home (Ich wohne in...), Beruf/profession (Ich bin...von Beruf), Familie, and Warum Deutsch (Ich lerne Deutsch, weil...). Cover all 6 to score maximum marks.
exercises.rule_hint: 6 introduction topics: Name · Origin · Home · Profession · Family · Why German.

lessons.title: Asking about others — and answering back
lessons.content: The Goethe Sprechen section is a conversation, not a monologue. The examiner asks you questions — and the second part involves you asking the examiner questions too. This lesson teaches both directions. In Part 2 of Sprechen, you must ask the examiner questions about pictures or about themselves. These are the W-question patterns you need: Read through this model conversation. Then cover the "You" lines and try to say them from memory before pressing 🔊.

exercises.question: "Woher kommen Sie?" — what is the correct answer structure?
exercises.hint: Woher = from where. The correct preposition matters.
exercises.explanation: "Ich komme aus Kenia." — correct. "Woher kommen Sie?" asks for origin, and the answer always uses "Ich komme aus" + country/city. "von" is wrong here. "Ich bin von" is a very common error — but "aus" is always correct for national/city origin.
exercises.rule_hint: Origin: Ich komme AUS [country/city]. Never "von" for country of origin.

exercises.question: In the Goethe Sprechen exam, you do not understand the examiner's question. What should you say?
exercises.hint: You need the examiner to repeat. There is a polite German way.
options: Wie bitte? / Könnten Sie das bitte wiederholen?
exercises.correct_answer: Wie bitte? / Könnten Sie das bitte wiederholen?
exercises.explanation: "Wie bitte?" or "Könnten Sie das bitte wiederholen?" are both correct and perfectly acceptable. The examiner expects you to request clarification when needed — this is part of communicative competence. "Was?" alone is too abrupt. Never switch to English.
exercises.rule_hint: Did not understand: "Wie bitte?" or "Könnten Sie das bitte wiederholen?" — always acceptable.

lessons.title: Saying goodbye — the right ending for every situation
lessons.content: How you end a conversation matters. In the Goethe Sprechen section, the final impression you leave — including your goodbye — is part of the examiner's overall assessment of your communicative competence.

exercises.question: You are ending a phone call with a German company. Which goodbye is correct?
exercises.hint: There is a specific goodbye only used on the phone
exercises.explanation: "Auf Wiederhören!" — the phone-specific goodbye. "Wieder-hören" = until we hear again (as opposed to "Wieder-sehen" = until we see again). Using "Auf Wiedersehen" on the phone is technically odd because you cannot "see" each other. "Auf Wiederhören" is the correct professional choice.
exercises.rule_hint: In person = Auf Wiedersehen. On the phone = Auf Wiederhören.

exercises.question: A shopkeeper hands you your change and says "Schönen Tag noch!" What does this mean?
exercises.hint: "noch" = still/yet — the day is continuing
exercises.explanation: "Schönen Tag noch!" = "Have a nice day still!" The "noch" implies the day is not over — it is a warm parting phrase said during the day when leaving a shop, office, or casual encounter. It is very common in Goethe Hören dialogues set in shops and service situations.
exercises.rule_hint: Schönen Tag noch! = Have a nice (rest of the) day! Common in shop and service contexts.

lessons.title: Polite phrases — the social glue of German
lessons.content: These phrases are short but they carry enormous social weight in German culture. Germans use them constantly and notice immediately when a foreigner does not. Using them correctly signals cultural awareness — and that impresses examiners.

exercises.question: What is the difference between "Entschuldigung" and "Es tut mir leid"?
exercises.hint: One is for attention, one is for a real apology
options: Entschuldigung = excuse me / to get attention. Es tut mir leid = genuine apology.
Es tut mir leid = excuse me. Entschuldigung = genuine apology.
Both are only used in formal situations.
exercises.correct_answer: Entschuldigung = excuse me / to get attention. Es tut mir leid = genuine apology.
exercises.explanation: "Entschuldigung" is for getting attention or passing by someone — like English "excuse me." "Es tut mir leid" is a genuine apology for something you did wrong — like English "I am truly sorry." Using "Es tut mir leid" when bumping past someone is slightly dramatic. Using "Entschuldigung" after a serious mistake is too casual.
exercises.rule_hint: Entschuldigung = excuse me (attention/passing). Es tut mir leid = I am sorry (genuine apology).

exercises.question: Someone says "Danke schön!" to you. What is the most appropriate formal response?
exercises.hint: There are two common options — choose the formal one
exercises.explanation: "Bitte schön!" or "Gern geschehen!" are both correct formal responses to "Danke schön." "Bitte schön" = here you are / you are welcome. "Gern geschehen" = gladly done / my pleasure. "Kein Problem" is acceptable but more casual.
exercises.rule_hint: Response to Danke: Bitte (schön)! or Gern geschehen! — both correct and natural.

exercises.question: How do you end the Goethe Sprechen exam when the examiner signals you are done?
exercises.hint: Formal, polite, complete — leaves a good impression
exercises.explanation: "Vielen Dank. Auf Wiedersehen!" — the perfect formal ending. "Vielen Dank" = thank you very much (for the time and the examination). "Auf Wiedersehen" = formal in-person goodbye. This is polite, professional, and complete. Never just "Tschüss" to an examiner.
exercises.rule_hint: End Goethe Sprechen with: Vielen Dank. Auf Wiedersehen!

title: haben and seinThe Two Verbs That Run German
description: Every sentence in German uses either haben or sein. They appear in every Goethe section — as main verbs, in the perfect tense, and in fixed expressions. Getting them wrong is the single most common grammar mistake in A1 exams.

lessons.title: haben — to have
lessons.content: haben means "to have." It expresses possession, relationships, and states. In German, haben is irregular — the conjugation does not follow normal verb patterns and must be memorised. Every form is used constantly in the Goethe exam.

exercises.question: Complete: "Ich ___ einen Hund."
exercises.hint: First person singular of haben
exercises.explanation: "Ich habe" — first person singular of haben. The forms: ich habe, du hast, er/sie/es hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie/Sie haben. "Ich bin" is from sein. "Ich hat" and "Ich hast" do not exist.
exercises.rule_hint: ich habe · du hast · er/sie/es hat · wir haben · ihr habt · sie/Sie haben

exercises.question: Your friend asks about his sister: "Was macht sie?" You say she has a car. Which is correct?
exercises.hint: Third person singular feminine — er/sie/es form
exercises.explanation: "Sie hat ein Auto." — er/sie/es hat. The third person singular of haben is "hat." "Sie habe" is wrong (ich form). "Sie haben" would mean "they have" or "you have" (formal). "Sie hast" does not exist.
exercises.rule_hint: er/sie/es → hat (third person singular of haben).

exercises.question: "___ ihr Zeit heute?" (Do you all have time today?)
exercises.hint: ihr = you all (plural informal)
exercises.explanation: "Habt ihr Zeit?" — ihr habt is the second person plural. In questions, the verb moves to first position: "Habt ihr...?" "Haben ihr" is wrong — haben is for wir/sie/Sie. "Hast" is du. "Hat" is er/sie/es.
exercises.rule_hint: ihr → habt. In questions: Habt ihr...?

lessons.title: sein — to be
lessons.content: sein means "to be." It is the most important verb in German and the most irregular. The forms look completely different from the infinitive — just like English "I am, you are, he is" look nothing like "to be." These forms must be memorised individually.

exercises.question: Complete: "Er ___ Arzt."
exercises.hint: Profession uses sein
exercises.explanation: "Er ist Arzt." — er/sie/es ist, from sein. Professions always use sein: Ich bin, du bist, er ist. "Er hat Arzt" is completely wrong — you cannot "have" a profession in German. "Er ist Arzt" = He is a doctor.
exercises.rule_hint: Professions: [subject] + sein (conjugated). er/sie/es → ist.

exercises.question: "Wir ___ sehr glücklich." (We are very happy.)
exercises.hint: Adjective describing a state — which verb?
exercises.explanation: "Wir sind sehr glücklich." — wir sind, from sein. Adjectives describing states use sein. glücklich (happy) describes what you are, not what you have. "Wir haben glücklich" is wrong.
exercises.rule_hint: sein + adjective = describing a state. wir → sind.

exercises.question: How do you ask "Are you (formal) married?" in German?
exercises.hint: Formal you = Sie. Verb goes first in questions.
exercises.explanation: "Sind Sie verheiratet?" — Sie sind → Sind Sie? (verb to front). verheiratet (married) is an adjective describing marital status, so sein is correct. "Haben Sie verheiratet?" is wrong. "Bist du verheiratet?" is correct but informal.
exercises.rule_hint: Formal question: Sind Sie [adjective]? Sie sind → Sind Sie?

lessons.title: haben vs sein — when to use which
lessons.content: This is where most learners get confused. Both verbs are used constantly, but they cover very different things. Once you understand the logic, the choice becomes clear.

exercises.question: "Ich ___ Hunger." (I am hungry.)
exercises.hint: Hunger is a noun in German — something you possess
exercises.explanation: "Ich habe Hunger." — German treats hunger as a noun you possess, not a state you are in. "Ich bin Hunger" is a very common mistake from English speakers. The same applies to Durst (thirst), Angst (fear), Zeit (time).
exercises.rule_hint: Physical needs: haben + noun. Hunger/Durst/Angst/Zeit = haben, NOT sein.

exercises.question: Which sentence uses the correct verb?
exercises.hint: Think about whether it is possession/identity or a state
exercises.explanation: "Sie ist Ärztin." — profession uses sein. "Ich bin ein Auto" = I am a car (wrong sense). "Ich habe müde" is wrong — müde is an adjective (state = sein): Ich bin müde. "Er haben Hunger" is wrong conjugation — er hat Hunger.
exercises.rule_hint: Profession = sein. Physical state adjective = sein. Possession/needs = haben.

exercises.question: "Das Konzert ___ um acht Uhr." (The concert is at eight o'clock.)
exercises.hint: Describing when something takes place — existence/state
exercises.explanation: "Das Konzert ist um acht Uhr." — sein describes when/where something exists or takes place. "Das Konzert hat um acht Uhr" would mean the concert has something at eight, which makes no sense.
exercises.rule_hint: Describing time/location of an event: sein. Das Konzert ist um...

lessons.title: The Perfect Tense with haben
lessons.content: In spoken German, the past is expressed using the Perfect tense — not the simple past. The Goethe A1 Sprechen section tests this. The perfect tense has two parts: a conjugated form of haben or sein, plus a past participle at the end of the sentence.

exercises.question: How do you say "I ate" in German using the perfect tense?
exercises.hint: essen → gegessen. Which auxiliary?
exercises.explanation: "Ich habe gegessen." — essen is an action done in one place (not movement), so it takes haben. The participle "gegessen" goes to the end. "Ich bin gegessen" uses the wrong auxiliary. "Ich habe essen" is wrong — the infinitive stays at the end only in other tenses, not the perfect.
exercises.rule_hint: Perfect with haben: [subject] + haben (conjugated) + ... + Partizip II (at end).

exercises.question: Where does the Partizip II go in a perfect tense sentence?
exercises.hint: German loves sending things to the end
options: At the very end of the sentence
exercises.correct_answer: At the very end of the sentence
exercises.explanation: The Partizip II always goes to the very end of the clause. "Ich habe gestern einen Brief geschrieben" — gestern (yesterday) and einen Brief (a letter) both come before geschrieben. This end-position rule is tested in both Schreiben and Sprechen.
exercises.rule_hint: Partizip II = always last. Everything else goes between haben/sein and the participle.

exercises.question: What is the Partizip II of "machen" (to do/make)?
exercises.hint: Regular verb: ge- + stem + -t
exercises.explanation: "gemacht" — regular verb pattern: ge + stem (mach) + t = gemacht. Regular verbs are predictable: kaufen→gekauft, spielen→gespielt, lernen→gelernt, machen→gemacht.
exercises.rule_hint: Regular Partizip II: ge- + verb stem + -t. Irregular ones must be memorised.

lessons.title: The Perfect Tense with sein
lessons.content: Most verbs form the perfect with haben. But a specific group uses sein instead. These are verbs of movement from A to B and verbs that describe a change of state. Once you know the rule, you can apply it to new verbs you learn.

exercises.question: "Ich ___ nach Hause gegangen." Which auxiliary?
exercises.hint: gehen = movement from A to B
exercises.explanation: "Ich bin nach Hause gegangen." — gehen (to go) is movement from A to B, so it takes sein. "Ich habe gegangen" is a very common error. Movement verbs: gehen, fahren, fliegen, kommen, laufen, reisen → all use sein in the perfect.
exercises.rule_hint: Movement A to B = sein in perfect. gehen → Ich bin gegangen.

exercises.question: Which verb uses sein in the perfect tense?
exercises.hint: Look for movement or change of state
exercises.explanation: "fliegen" (to fly) — movement from one place to another → sein. Ich bin geflogen. essen, arbeiten, kaufen are all actions done in one place → haben. Ich habe gegessen / gearbeitet / gekauft.
exercises.rule_hint: Movement/change of state = sein. All other actions = haben.

exercises.question: "Sie ___ um sechs Uhr aufgewacht." (She woke up at six.)
exercises.hint: aufwachen = change of state (asleep → awake)
exercises.explanation: "Sie ist um sechs Uhr aufgewacht." — aufwachen (to wake up) is a change of state (from sleeping to awake), so it uses sein. She changed condition. er/sie/es + sein = ist. "Sie hat aufgewacht" is a common error.
exercises.rule_hint: Change of state = sein. aufwachen, einschlafen, werden → sein in perfect.

lessons.title: Fixed expressions — what Goethe actually tests
lessons.content: The Goethe exam does not test grammar in isolation. It tests grammar inside real sentences. These fixed expressions using haben and sein appear repeatedly across all four Goethe sections. Knowing them cold means free marks.

exercises.question: How do you say "I am right" in German?
exercises.hint: This is a fixed expression — which verb does German use?
exercises.explanation: "Ich habe Recht." — "Recht haben" is a fixed expression in German. Recht is a noun meaning "right/correctness." You possess being right. "Ich bin Recht" is wrong. "Ich bin richtig" means "I am correct" (about a fact) but "Ich habe Recht" is the standard expression.
exercises.rule_hint: Recht haben = to be right. Unrecht haben = to be wrong. Always haben.

exercises.question: "Das ___ kein Problem." (That is not a problem.)
exercises.hint: Describing what something IS
exercises.explanation: "Das ist kein Problem." — sein describes what something is. "kein" negates a noun (Problem). "Das hat kein Problem" would mean "That does not have a problem" — grammatically odd and not the right expression. "Das ist kein Problem" is the standard social response.
exercises.rule_hint: Das ist kein Problem. — essential social phrase. Always sein.

exercises.question: Which is correct for "We have the day off"?
exercises.hint: Think about frei as something you possess
exercises.explanation: "Wir haben morgen frei." — "frei haben" is a fixed expression meaning to have a day off / to be free. "morgen" (tomorrow) goes between haben and frei. "Wir sind morgen frei" is also acceptable (we are free tomorrow) but "frei haben" is the standard expression for having time off work.
exercises.rule_hint: frei haben = to have a day off. Fixed expression — always haben.

title: der · die · dasNouns and Gender
description: Every German noun has a gender — masculine, feminine, or neuter. The article (der, die, das) tells you the gender. This is not optional information. Gender controls the article, the adjective endings, and the case system. Get the gender wrong and every sentence about that noun is wrong.

lessons.title: The three genders — masculine, feminine, neuter
lessons.content: In German, every noun is one of three genders. The gender is shown by the definite article before the noun. Unlike biological gender, grammatical gender in German is not always logical — a girl (das Mädchen) is neuter, a key (der Schlüssel) is masculine. You must learn the article with every noun.

exercises.question: What article does "Haus" (house) take?
exercises.hint: Think about common neuter nouns
exercises.explanation: "das Haus" — house is neuter in German. This must be memorised. Haus does not follow an obvious ending rule — it is one of the core neuter nouns you learn by heart. das Haus / die Häuser (plural with umlaut).
exercises.rule_hint: das Haus (neuter). Learn article with every noun — never Haus alone.

exercises.question: What is the indefinite article (a/an) for a feminine noun?
exercises.hint: Der/die/das → ein/eine/ein
exercises.explanation: "eine" — feminine nouns take "eine" as the indefinite article. Masculine and neuter both take "ein." So: ein Mann (m), eine Frau (f), ein Kind (n). The feminine "eine" is the only form that looks different.
exercises.rule_hint: Indefinite: masculine=ein · feminine=eine · neuter=ein.

exercises.question: "Das Mädchen" — why is "girl" neuter in German?
exercises.hint: Think about the ending, not the biology
options: Because girls are considered neuter in German culture
It is a mistake — it should be die Mädchen
Because Mädchen means child, not girl
exercises.explanation: Nouns ending in -chen or -lein (diminutive suffixes) are always neuter — regardless of what they refer to. das Mädchen (girl), das Häuschen (little house), das Kindlein (little child). The suffix overrules the biological gender. This is one of the most famous gender traps in German.
exercises.rule_hint: -chen and -lein endings = always das (neuter). Biology does not matter.

lessons.title: Gender rules — patterns you can use right now
lessons.content: You cannot memorise the gender of every German noun. But you can learn the patterns that predict gender most of the time. These rules cover the majority of the vocabulary in Goethe A1.

exercises.question: The noun "die Wohnung" ends in -ung. What does this tell you?
exercises.hint: There is a rule for -ung endings
options: It could be any gender — ung endings vary
It is definitely feminine — all -ung nouns are die
exercises.correct_answer: It is definitely feminine — all -ung nouns are die
exercises.explanation: All German nouns ending in -ung are feminine — die. No exceptions. Wohnung, Übung, Zeitung, Öffnungszeit, Entschuldigung, Erklärung — every single one is die. Memorise this one rule and you correctly gender dozens of common nouns automatically.
exercises.rule_hint: -ung ending = always die. Zero exceptions.

exercises.question: What gender are nouns ending in -heit and -keit?
exercises.hint: Abstract noun endings have patterns
options: It varies — must check each one
exercises.explanation: -heit and -keit endings are always feminine — die. die Freiheit (freedom), die Möglichkeit (possibility), die Freundlichkeit (friendliness), die Gesundheit (health). These abstract noun suffixes are reliable gender signals.
exercises.rule_hint: -heit and -keit = always die (feminine).

exercises.question: A noun ends in -chen. What article does it take?
exercises.hint: Diminutive suffixes have a fixed rule
exercises.explanation: "das" — all diminutives ending in -chen are neuter. das Mädchen, das Häuschen, das Hündchen. The diminutive suffix overrides all other gender signals, including biological gender. This rule has no exceptions.
exercises.rule_hint: -chen ending = always das (neuter).

lessons.title: Home and daily life — the nouns Goethe tests most
lessons.content: These are the nouns that appear most frequently in Goethe A1 Hören and Lesen. Learn each one with its colour-coded article. Tap any card to hear the German.

exercises.question: What article does "Küche" (kitchen) take?
exercises.hint: Kitchen is a room — feminine
exercises.explanation: "die Küche" — kitchen is feminine. Many room and building words are feminine: die Küche, die Schule, die Apotheke, die Universität. This must be memorised for Küche specifically, but the -e ending combined with knowing it is a place helps.
exercises.rule_hint: die Küche (feminine). Learn with article: die Küche / die Küchen (plural).

exercises.question: What article does "Bahnhof" (train station) take?
exercises.hint: Think about der Hof — the second part of a compound noun often predicts the gender
exercises.explanation: "der Bahnhof" — train station is masculine. In German compound nouns, the LAST part determines the gender. Bahn (train/track) + Hof (yard/court) → der Hof is masculine → der Bahnhof. This compound rule works for most compound nouns.
exercises.rule_hint: Compound nouns: the LAST part determines the gender. der Hof → der Bahnhof.

exercises.question: Which article goes with "Frühstück" (breakfast)?
exercises.hint: -stück comes from das Stück (piece) — compound rule applies
exercises.explanation: "das Frühstück" — breakfast is neuter. früh (early) + Stück (piece/bit) → das Stück is neuter → das Frühstück. The compound noun rule again: the last component (Stück) is neuter, so Frühstück is neuter. Similarly: das Mittagessen, das Abendessen (das Essen is neuter).
exercises.rule_hint: das Frühstück / das Mittagessen / das Abendessen — all neuter (das Essen).

lessons.title: Work, people, and places — tap to hear each

exercises.question: What article does "Kind" (child) take?
exercises.hint: This is one of the most important nouns in A1
exercises.explanation: "das Kind" — child is neuter. This is a core vocabulary item that every A1 student must know. Plural: die Kinder. "Ich habe zwei Kinder" — always das Kind, die Kinder.
exercises.rule_hint: das Kind / die Kinder. Core A1 noun — must be automatic.

exercises.question: A female teacher is "die Lehrerin." Why does it end in -in?
exercises.hint: -in is the feminine suffix for professions
options: Because -in is added to masculine forms to make the feminine
Because Lehrerin is a different word from Lehrer
Because teaching is a traditionally female profession
exercises.correct_answer: Because -in is added to masculine forms to make the feminine
exercises.explanation: In German, the feminine form of most professions is made by adding -in to the masculine form. der Lehrer → die Lehrerin. der Arzt → die Ärztin (with umlaut). der Student → die Studentin. der Ingenieur → die Ingenieurin. The -in suffix is a reliable signal that a profession noun is feminine.
exercises.rule_hint: Masculine profession + -in = feminine. Lehrer→Lehrerin, Arzt→Ärztin.

exercises.question: What article does "Krankenhaus" (hospital) take?
exercises.hint: Use the compound noun rule — what is the last part?
exercises.explanation: "das Krankenhaus" — hospital is neuter. krank (sick) + Haus (house) → das Haus is neuter → das Krankenhaus. The compound rule works perfectly here. You already know das Haus — so you automatically know das Krankenhaus.
exercises.rule_hint: das Haus → das Krankenhaus. Compound rule: last part determines gender.

lessons.title: Plural forms — all plurals use die
lessons.content: The good news: every German noun, regardless of its singular gender, uses die in the plural. der Tisch → die Tische. das Kind → die Kinder. die Frau → die Frauen. The article is always die in the plural. The hard part is that the noun itself changes — and there are five different plural patterns. You must learn the plural form of each noun along with its gender.

exercises.question: What article do ALL German nouns take in the plural?
exercises.hint: This is the one consistent plural rule
exercises.explanation: All German nouns take "die" in the plural — regardless of their singular gender. der Mann → die Männer. die Frau → die Frauen. das Kind → die Kinder. The plural always uses die. This is one of the most helpful simplifications in German grammar.
exercises.rule_hint: ALL plurals = die. der/die/das in singular → die in plural.

exercises.question: What is the plural of "das Auto"?
exercises.hint: Auto is a loanword — which plural pattern do loanwords use?
exercises.explanation: "die Autos" — foreign loanwords and abbreviations typically add -s in the plural. das Auto → die Autos. das Kino → die Kinos. das Café → die Cafés. das Handy → die Handys. When in doubt with a clearly foreign word, -s plural is usually correct.
exercises.rule_hint: Loanwords → -s plural. Auto→Autos, Kino→Kinos, Handy→Handys.

exercises.question: What is the plural of "der Lehrer" (teacher)?
exercises.hint: -er nouns often have no change in the plural
exercises.explanation: "die Lehrer" — no change in the plural. Many masculine nouns ending in -er (and all nouns ending in -chen and -lein) have identical singular and plural forms. Only the article changes: der Lehrer → die Lehrer. You tell the difference from context or the article.
exercises.rule_hint: -er nouns often unchanged in plural. der Lehrer → die Lehrer.

lessons.title: Gender in real Goethe sentences
lessons.content: The Goethe exam tests gender in several ways: form-filling (writing the correct article), Lesen (choosing the right sentence), and Schreiben (producing sentences). These are the patterns that appear most often. The first Schreiben task is a form where you fill in personal information. Gender appears here because German forms use articles before categories.

exercises.question: "Die ___ beginnt um neun Uhr." (The opening time begins at nine.) Which noun fits?
exercises.hint: Think about the -zeit ending — what gender does Zeit carry?
exercises.explanation: "die Öffnungszeit" — Öffnung (opening) + Zeit (time) → die Zeit is feminine → die Öffnungszeit. The compound rule applies: the last part (Zeit) is feminine, so the whole compound is feminine. This appears constantly in Goethe Hören contexts about shop hours.
exercises.rule_hint: die Zeit → die Öffnungszeit. Compound rule: last part (Zeit) = feminine.

exercises.question: On a German form, you see "Datum: ___". What article does Datum take?
exercises.hint: Datum ends in -um — what gender pattern does that suggest?
exercises.explanation: "das Datum" — nouns ending in -um are neuter. das Datum, das Zentrum, das Museum, das Argument. The -um ending is a reliable neuter signal. This appears on every Goethe form-filling task — knowing das Datum helps you understand what information is being asked for.
exercises.rule_hint: -um ending = neuter (das). das Datum, das Zentrum, das Museum.

exercises.question: You see "die Wohnung hat drei ___." (The apartment has three ___.) Which plural is correct?
exercises.hint: Zimmer is a -er noun — what happens to its plural?
exercises.explanation: "drei Zimmer" — das Zimmer is a neuter -er noun whose plural is identical to the singular. "drei Zimmer" not "drei Zimmer+n." When counting nouns directly after a number, you use the plural form without the article: drei Zimmer, zwei Kinder, vier Tische.
exercises.rule_hint: das Zimmer → die Zimmer (no change). Neuter -er nouns often unchanged.

title: The Akkusativ CaseObjects in German
description: The Akkusativ is the first case change you encounter in German. It affects the article in front of a noun when that noun is the object of a verb. Only one article actually changes — but getting it right or wrong is the difference between a correct and incorrect sentence every time you use a transitive verb.

lessons.title: Subject vs Object — why the article changes
lessons.content: In German, the article in front of a noun changes to show its role in the sentence. The Nominativ (subject) is who is doing the action. The Akkusativ (direct object) is what is being acted upon. In English, word order tells you who is doing what. "The dog bites the man" vs "The man bites the dog" — same words, different meaning, because of position. In German, cases carry that information. So German can rearrange words for emphasis — the case endings signal the role, not the position.

exercises.question: In "Der Hund beißt den Mann" — what role is "den Mann" playing?
exercises.hint: den = Akkusativ. What does Akkusativ signal?
exercises.explanation: "den Mann" is the direct object — being bitten. "den" (Akkusativ of der) signals object. "Der Hund" (Nominativ = der, unchanged) is the subject doing the biting. The case endings tell you the role, regardless of word order.
exercises.rule_hint: Nominativ (der/die/das) = subject. Akkusativ (den/die/das) = direct object.

exercises.question: Which sentence has a direct object in the Akkusativ?
exercises.hint: Look for a verb that acts on something
exercises.explanation: "Ich kaufe einen Kaffee." — kaufen is a transitive verb, and "einen Kaffee" is its direct object (Akkusativ). "Ich gehe" and "Es regnet" have no direct objects. "Ich schlafe gut" — gut is an adverb, not an object.
exercises.rule_hint: Transitive verbs (kaufen, sehen, essen...) take direct objects → Akkusativ.

exercises.question: Why does German use cases like Nominativ and Akkusativ?
exercises.hint: Think about word order flexibility
options: To make German harder to learn
To show the role of a noun regardless of word order
To replace verbs in certain sentences
exercises.correct_answer: To show the role of a noun regardless of word order
exercises.explanation: Cases show the role of a noun (subject, object, etc.) through the article ending rather than word order. This gives German flexible word order — "Den Mann beißt der Hund" is as clear as "Der Hund beißt den Mann" because "den" always signals object.
exercises.rule_hint: Cases = noun role markers. German uses cases instead of strict word order.

lessons.title: The article table — what changes and what stays
lessons.content: This table is the core of the Akkusativ. Study it until you can produce the correct form without thinking. The orange cells are the changes — everything else stays the same as the Nominativ.

exercises.question: What is the Akkusativ form of "der Bus"?
exercises.hint: Only masculine articles change in Akkusativ
exercises.explanation: "den Bus" — masculine definite article der becomes den in the Akkusativ. This is the only change: der → den. "Nehmen Sie den Bus" = Take the bus (Bus is the object of nehmen).
exercises.rule_hint: Masculine definite: der (Nom) → den (Akk). Only masculine changes.

exercises.question: "Ich kaufe ___ Apfel." (Apfel is masculine — der Apfel)
exercises.hint: Indefinite article, masculine, in the Akkusativ
exercises.explanation: "einen Apfel" — the indefinite masculine article ein becomes einen in the Akkusativ. This mirrors the definite change: der→den, ein→einen. The -en ending signals masculine Akkusativ.
exercises.rule_hint: Masculine indefinite: ein (Nom) → einen (Akk). Pattern: add -en.

exercises.question: "Ich sehe ___ Frau." Which article is correct?
exercises.hint: Frau is feminine — what happens to die in Akkusativ?
exercises.explanation: "die Frau" — feminine articles do NOT change in the Akkusativ. die stays die. eine stays eine. Only the masculine article changes (der→den, ein→einen). This is the key fact: memorise that feminine, neuter, and plural stay the same.
exercises.rule_hint: Feminine: die (Nom) → die (Akk). No change. Only masculine changes.

lessons.title: Verbs that take the Akkusativ
lessons.content: Transitive verbs — verbs that pass their action onto an object — take the Akkusativ. These are the most common action verbs in German. When you use any of these verbs, the direct object takes the Akkusativ article. Tap each card to hear a sentence.

exercises.question: Which verb always takes a direct object in the Akkusativ?
exercises.hint: Which of these can you "do" to something?
exercises.explanation: "kaufen" (to buy) is a transitive verb — you always buy something, and that something takes the Akkusativ. gehen, schlafen, and ankommen are intransitive — they describe an action or state without a direct object.
exercises.rule_hint: Transitive verbs = take Akkusativ objects. Ask "what?" after the verb — if there is an answer, it takes Akkusativ.

exercises.question: "Ich nehme ___ Zug." (Zug is masculine — der Zug)
exercises.hint: nehmen = to take. Zug = object of taking.
exercises.explanation: "den Zug" — nehmen is a transitive verb. Der Zug becomes den Zug in the Akkusativ (masculine: der→den). "Ich nehme den Zug" = I take the train. Very common in Goethe Hören transport contexts.
exercises.rule_hint: nehmen + Akkusativ. Masculine: den. Ich nehme den Zug/Bus/Lift.

exercises.question: "Sie besucht ___ Arzt." (Arzt is masculine — der Arzt)
exercises.hint: besuchen = to visit. Arzt is the object being visited.
exercises.explanation: "einen Arzt" — besuchen takes Akkusativ. If using the indefinite article: ein → einen (masculine Akkusativ). "Sie besucht einen Arzt" = She visits a doctor. With definite: "Sie besucht den Arzt" (the specific doctor she knows).
exercises.rule_hint: besuchen + Akkusativ. einen (indef.) / den (def.) for masculine nouns.

lessons.title: Negation — kein and nicht
lessons.content: German has two ways to say "no/not" — kein and nicht. Choosing the wrong one is one of the most common A1 mistakes. The rule is simple once you see it. kein follows the same pattern as ein/eine/ein — so in the Akkusativ, masculine kein adds -en just like ein → einen.

exercises.question: "Ich habe ___ Auto." (negated — I have no car)
exercises.hint: Auto is neuter. kein negates nouns.
exercises.explanation: "kein Auto" — Auto is neuter (das Auto). kein for neuter is "kein" in both Nominativ and Akkusativ — no change. "Ich habe nicht Auto" is wrong — nicht does not negate nouns directly. kein replaces ein before nouns.
exercises.rule_hint: Neuter noun negation: kein Auto (no -en change for neuter).

exercises.question: "Ich habe ___ Bruder." (negated — I have no brother)
exercises.hint: Bruder is masculine. kein in the Akkusativ for masculine?
exercises.explanation: "keinen Bruder" — Bruder is masculine (der Bruder). In the Akkusativ, masculine kein adds -en: keinen. This mirrors ein → einen. "Ich habe keinen Bruder" = I have no brother / I don't have a brother.
exercises.rule_hint: Masculine Akkusativ negation: kein → keinen. Mirrors ein → einen.

exercises.question: When do you use "nicht" instead of "kein"?
exercises.hint: kein replaces ein before nouns. What does nicht negate?
options: Before verbs, adjectives, or nouns with definite articles
exercises.correct_answer: Before verbs, adjectives, or nouns with definite articles
exercises.explanation: nicht negates verbs ("Ich arbeite nicht"), adjectives ("Das ist nicht gut"), and nouns with definite articles ("Ich nehme nicht den Bus — I'm not taking that specific bus"). kein negates nouns that would take ein/eine/ein or no article at all.
exercises.rule_hint: kein = negates nouns (replaces ein). nicht = negates verbs, adjectives, or definite-article nouns.

lessons.title: Akkusativ prepositions — always den, never der
lessons.content: Certain prepositions always take the Akkusativ. This means the noun after them always uses the Akkusativ article — even if it would otherwise be the subject of a sentence. Memorise this list. It is short and fixed.

exercises.question: Which preposition always takes the Akkusativ?
exercises.hint: Think: durch für gegen ohne um
exercises.explanation: "für" always takes the Akkusativ. The five Akkusativ prepositions are: durch, für, gegen, ohne, um. "mit" takes Dativ. "in" and "auf" are two-way prepositions (Akkusativ for direction, Dativ for location).
exercises.rule_hint: Akkusativ prepositions: durch · für · gegen · ohne · um. Always.

exercises.question: "Das Geschenk ist für ___ Mann." (the specific man)
exercises.hint: für takes Akkusativ. Mann is masculine.
exercises.explanation: "für den Mann" — für takes Akkusativ. Mann is masculine → der becomes den in Akkusativ. "für den Mann" = for the man. The preposition für forces Akkusativ regardless of where the noun appears in the sentence.
exercises.rule_hint: für + Akkusativ always. Masculine definite: für den.

exercises.question: "Ohne ___ Schlüssel kann ich nicht rein." (without the key)
exercises.hint: ohne takes Akkusativ. Schlüssel is masculine.
exercises.explanation: "ohne den Schlüssel" — ohne takes Akkusativ. Schlüssel is masculine (der Schlüssel) → den Schlüssel. "Without the key I can't get in." The Akkusativ preposition ohne forces den for masculine nouns.
exercises.rule_hint: ohne + Akkusativ. Masculine: ohne den. Feminine: ohne die. Neuter: ohne das.

lessons.title: Akkusativ in real Goethe sentences
lessons.content: The Akkusativ appears in every section of the Goethe exam. These are the real sentence patterns the examiner uses. Read, hear, and internalise each one.

exercises.question: "Ich möchte ___ Termin machen." (an appointment)
exercises.hint: möchte takes Akkusativ. Termin is masculine.
exercises.explanation: "einen Termin" — möchte (would like) takes Akkusativ on its object. Termin is masculine (der Termin) → einen Termin (indefinite Akkusativ). "Ich möchte einen Termin machen" is the standard phrase for booking appointments in Goethe Schreiben.
exercises.rule_hint: Möchte + Akkusativ. Masculine indefinite: einen. Standard phrase: einen Termin machen.

exercises.question: "Nehmen Sie ___ Bus Nummer acht." Which is correct?
exercises.hint: nehmen takes Akkusativ. Bus is masculine.
exercises.explanation: "den Bus" — nehmen takes Akkusativ. Bus is masculine → den Bus. This is a definite article (the specific bus number 8). Appears constantly in Goethe Hören directions. "Nehmen Sie den Bus" = Take the bus.
exercises.rule_hint: Nehmen Sie den Bus/Zug/Lift. Masculine Akkusativ: den.

exercises.question: What does the article "den" tell you when you hear it in a Goethe listening text?
exercises.hint: Think about what Akkusativ signals
options: The noun is the subject doing the action
The noun is a direct object or follows an Akkusativ preposition
The noun is in the plural
exercises.correct_answer: The noun is a direct object or follows an Akkusativ preposition
exercises.explanation: "den" signals masculine Akkusativ — the noun is either a direct object or follows an Akkusativ preposition (durch/für/gegen/ohne/um). This is a comprehension shortcut: when you hear "den," you know immediately that noun is being acted upon, not acting.
exercises.rule_hint: "den" = masculine Akkusativ. Signals direct object or Akkusativ preposition.

title: The Dativ CaseThe Indirect Object
description: The Dativ is the third case. Where the Akkusativ marked the direct object (what is acted on), the Dativ marks the indirect object — the recipient, the beneficiary, the person something is done for or given to. It also appears after a fixed set of prepositions.

lessons.title: The indirect object — to whom, for whom
lessons.content: The Dativ marks the indirect object — the person or thing that receives the direct object. The simplest test: ask "to whom?" or "for whom?" after the verb. That answer takes the Dativ. Notice: the sentence has three noun roles at once. "Ich" is the subject (Nominativ). "Einen Kaffee" is what is given (Akkusativ). "Dem Mann" is the recipient — to whom it is given (Dativ). German handles all three with different articles.

exercises.question: In "Ich gebe dem Kind einen Apfel" — what is the Dativ?
exercises.hint: Ask: to whom am I giving?
exercises.explanation: "dem Kind" is the Dativ — the indirect object. To whom do I give? → to the child. "dem" is the neuter Dativ article (das Kind → dem Kind). "einen Apfel" is Akkusativ — what I give.
exercises.rule_hint: Dativ = indirect object. Ask "to whom?" or "for whom?" after the verb.

exercises.question: How do you identify the indirect object in a sentence?
exercises.hint: What question do you ask?
options: Ask "to whom?" or "for whom?" after the verb
Look for the longest noun phrase
Look for the noun closest to the verb
exercises.correct_answer: Ask "to whom?" or "for whom?" after the verb
exercises.explanation: Ask "to whom?" or "for whom?" after the verb. "Ich schreibe meiner Mutter eine E-Mail" — to whom do I write? → meiner Mutter (Dativ). What do I write? → eine E-Mail (Akkusativ).
exercises.rule_hint: Indirect object test: ask "to whom?" / "for whom?" → answer takes Dativ.

exercises.question: "Wie geht es Ihnen?" — why is "Ihnen" in the Dativ?
exercises.hint: Ihnen is the Dativ form of Sie (formal you)
options: Because "gehen" takes Dativ in this expression
exercises.correct_answer: Because "gehen" takes Dativ in this expression
exercises.explanation: "Wie geht es Ihnen?" is a fixed expression where "es geht" takes Dativ. "Ihnen" is the formal Dativ of "Sie." The full structure: "Wie geht es + Dativ?" — this is why the response is also Dativ: "Danke, gut. Und Ihnen?"
exercises.rule_hint: "Wie geht es Ihnen?" — Ihnen = formal Dativ of Sie. Fixed expression.

lessons.title: The Dativ article forms — dem · der · dem
lessons.content: Unlike the Akkusativ (only masculine changes), the Dativ changes masculine, feminine, and neuter. Learn the four Dativ forms and place them alongside what you already know.

exercises.question: What is the Dativ form of "der Mann" (masculine)?
exercises.hint: Definite article, masculine, Dativ
exercises.explanation: "dem Mann" — masculine Dativ is dem. The three Dativ definite forms: dem (masculine), der (feminine), dem (neuter). Masculine and neuter share dem. Note: "den" is Akkusativ masculine, not Dativ.
exercises.rule_hint: Masculine Dativ: dem. Full Dativ: dem · der · dem · den (plural).

exercises.question: What is the Dativ of "die Frau" (feminine)?
exercises.hint: Feminine Dativ looks like masculine Nominativ — do not confuse them
exercises.explanation: "der Frau" — feminine Dativ is der. This is the famous trap: der is both masculine Nominativ AND feminine Dativ. Context tells you which: "Der Mann kommt" = masculine subject. "Ich helfe der Frau" = feminine Dativ object of helfen.
exercises.rule_hint: Feminine Dativ: der. Same spelling as masculine Nominativ — context distinguishes them.

exercises.question: What is the Dativ pronoun for "ich"?
exercises.hint: Dativ pronouns are different from Nominativ and Akkusativ forms
exercises.explanation: "mir" — the Dativ of ich is mir. Nominativ: ich. Akkusativ: mich. Dativ: mir. "Kannst du mir helfen?" = Can you help me? "Wie geht es mir?" = How am I doing? "Das gehört mir." = That belongs to me.
exercises.rule_hint: ich → mir (Dativ). du → dir. er → ihm. sie → ihr. Sie → Ihnen. wir → uns.

lessons.title: Verbs that always take the Dativ
lessons.content: Most verbs that take an object take the Akkusativ. But a specific group of verbs always takes the Dativ — even though they seem like normal action verbs. These must be memorised. They appear constantly in Goethe.

exercises.question: "Ich helfe ___ Arzt." Which form is correct?
exercises.hint: helfen always takes Dativ. Arzt is masculine.
exercises.explanation: "dem Arzt" — helfen always takes Dativ. Arzt is masculine (der Arzt) → dem Arzt in Dativ. "Ich helfe den Arzt" uses Akkusativ, which is wrong for helfen. This is the single most tested Dativ verb.
exercises.rule_hint: helfen + Dativ always. Masculine: helfen dem. "Ich helfe dem Arzt."

exercises.question: "Das Lied gefällt ___ sehr." (The song pleases me / I like the song very much.)
exercises.hint: gefallen takes Dativ for the person who is pleased
exercises.explanation: "mir" — gefallen takes Dativ for the person experiencing the feeling. The structure is reversed from English: "Das Lied gefällt mir" = literally "The song pleases me." The song (das Lied) is Nominativ subject. I (mir) am Dativ. "Ich gefalle das Lied" is completely wrong.
exercises.rule_hint: gefallen + Dativ. Das gefällt mir. = I like that. Reversed from English structure.

exercises.question: Which of these verbs takes the Dativ — not the Akkusativ?
exercises.hint: Most action verbs take Akkusativ. One of these is different.
exercises.explanation: "helfen" takes Dativ. kaufen, sehen, and lesen all take Akkusativ (they have direct objects: buy what, see what, read what). helfen is different — you help a person, and that person takes Dativ in German.
exercises.rule_hint: Dativ verbs: helfen, danken, gefallen, gehören, antworten. Must be memorised.

lessons.title: Dativ prepositions — always dem, never den
lessons.content: These prepositions always take the Dativ — no exceptions, no context dependency. After any of these prepositions, every noun takes the Dativ article. Tap each to hear an example sentence.

exercises.question: "Ich fahre ___ Bus." (I travel by bus.) Which preposition and article?
exercises.hint: Transport uses mit + Dativ
exercises.explanation: "mit dem Bus" — mit takes Dativ. Bus is masculine (der Bus) → dem Bus. "mit dem Bus fahren" = to travel by bus. Similarly: mit dem Zug (by train), mit dem Auto (by car), mit der U-Bahn (by metro — feminine).
exercises.rule_hint: Transport: mit + Dativ. mit dem Bus/Zug/Auto. mit der U-Bahn/Straßenbahn.

exercises.question: "Ich gehe ___ Arzt." (I am going to the doctor.) Which contraction?
exercises.hint: zu + dem contracts to a single word
exercises.explanation: "zum Arzt" — zu + dem = zum (mandatory contraction). Arzt is masculine → dem Arzt → zum Arzt. "zu dem Arzt" sounds unnatural and marks you as a learner. Always contract: zum (masc/neut) and zur (fem): zum Arzt, zur Schule, zum Krankenhaus.
exercises.rule_hint: zu + dem → zum. zu + der → zur. Mandatory in natural German.

exercises.question: What does "seit" mean, and what tense does it use?
exercises.hint: seit describes something ongoing — what tense signals ongoing action?
exercises.explanation: "seit" means "since" or "for" and describes something that started in the past and is still ongoing NOW. It always uses the present tense in German. "Ich lerne seit zwei Jahren Deutsch" = I have been learning German for two years (and I still am). Using past tense with seit is a grammatical error.
exercises.rule_hint: seit + present tense = ongoing action. "Ich lerne seit zwei Jahren Deutsch."

lessons.title: Two-way prepositions — movement or location?
lessons.content: Nine prepositions can take either Akkusativ or Dativ depending on meaning. The rule is logical and consistent: Akkusativ signals movement toward a destination. Dativ signals location — being somewhere.

exercises.question: "Ich gehe ___ Küche." (I am going into the kitchen.) Which article?
exercises.hint: Movement toward destination = Akkusativ. Küche is feminine.
exercises.explanation: "in die Küche" — going into = movement toward destination → Akkusativ. Küche is feminine (die Küche) → die Küche (feminine Akkusativ unchanged). "in der Küche" would mean being in the kitchen (location = Dativ).
exercises.rule_hint: Movement (wohin?) = Akkusativ. in die Küche (going in). in der Küche (being there).

exercises.question: "Das Buch liegt ___ Tisch." (The book is lying on the table.) Which form?
exercises.hint: Lying = location, not movement. Tisch is masculine.
exercises.explanation: "auf dem Tisch" — lying = location (wo?) → Dativ. Tisch is masculine (der Tisch) → dem Tisch. "auf den Tisch" would mean putting something onto the table (movement → Akkusativ). "Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch" vs "Ich lege das Buch auf den Tisch."
exercises.rule_hint: Location (wo?) = Dativ. auf dem Tisch. Movement (wohin?) = Akkusativ. auf den Tisch.

exercises.question: "Der Kurs ist ___ Montag." Which contraction?
exercises.hint: an + dem contracts. Montag is masculine.
exercises.explanation: "am Montag" — an + dem = am (mandatory contraction). Days of the week (all masculine: der Montag) always use "am" in time expressions. "am Montag" = on Monday. "am Wochenende" = at the weekend. "an dem Montag" sounds unnatural.
exercises.rule_hint: an + dem → am. Time with days: am Montag, am Dienstag... am Wochenende.

lessons.title: Dativ in real Goethe sentences
lessons.content: These are the sentence patterns where the Dativ actually appears in the exam. Read, hear, and understand why each Dativ form is correct.

exercises.question: "Ich fahre ___ Zug nach München." Which is correct?
exercises.hint: mit takes Dativ. Zug is masculine.
exercises.explanation: "mit dem Zug" — mit takes Dativ. Zug is masculine (der Zug) → dem Zug. "Ich fahre mit dem Zug nach München" = I travel to Munich by train. nach + city (no article) also takes Dativ but cities without articles show no visible change.
exercises.rule_hint: mit + Dativ for transport. Masculine: mit dem. mit dem Zug/Bus/Auto.

exercises.question: "Das Geschäft ist ___ Montag bis ___ Freitag geöffnet."
exercises.hint: von/bis zu + Dativ. Both need the same contraction.
exercises.explanation: "vom Montag bis zum Freitag" — von + dem = vom. zu + dem = zum. Both are mandatory contractions. This opening-hours structure appears in every Goethe Lesen task about shops and services. "von dem ... zu dem" sounds unnatural and marks you as a learner.
exercises.rule_hint: von + dem → vom. zu + dem → zum. Vom Montag bis zum Freitag.

exercises.question: Which sentence uses ALL three cases correctly?
exercises.hint: Check: subject (Nom), direct object (Akk), indirect object/prep (Dat)
options: Ich gebe dem Kind einen Apfel.
Ich gebe den Kind ein Apfel.
Ich gebe der Kind einen Apfel.
Ich gebe dem Kind ein Apfels.
exercises.correct_answer: Ich gebe dem Kind einen Apfel.
exercises.explanation: "Ich gebe dem Kind einen Apfel." — Ich = Nominativ (subject). dem Kind = Dativ (neuter: das Kind → dem Kind, indirect object). einen Apfel = Akkusativ (masculine: der Apfel → einen Apfel, direct object). All three cases correct in one sentence.
exercises.rule_hint: Three cases in one: Ich (Nom) gebe dem Kind (Dat) einen Apfel (Akk).

title: Sentence Structure& Word Order
description: German word order is rule-based and logical — but the rules are different from English. The verb has a fixed position. Time, manner, and place follow a sequence. Subordinate clauses send the verb to the end. Master these rules and your sentences will sound natural in every Goethe section.

lessons.title: The verb always occupies position 2
lessons.content: In German main clauses, the conjugated verb is always the second element. Not the second word — the second unit of meaning. The first element can be the subject, a time expression, a place, or any other phrase. Whatever it is, the verb comes immediately after it.

exercises.question: In German main clauses, where does the conjugated verb go?
exercises.hint: This is the single most important word order rule
exercises.explanation: The conjugated verb always goes in position 2 of a German main clause. Not second word — second element. Whatever comes first (subject, time, place), the verb is immediately after it. This rule is called "Verb-Zweit" (V2).
exercises.rule_hint: German main clause: V2. Whatever is first, verb is always second element.

exercises.question: Which sentence has the correct German word order?
exercises.hint: Find the one where the verb is the second element
exercises.explanation: "Ich gehe heute zum Arzt." — Ich (P1) + gehe (P2, verb) + heute (time) + zum Arzt (place). The verb is the second element. "Heute ich gehe" is wrong — after "Heute" (P1), the verb must be P2, so "gehe" must come before "ich".
exercises.rule_hint: Verb in P2: Ich gehe... or Heute gehe ich... — verb always second element.

exercises.question: "Am Montag ___ der Kurs." Which word order is correct?
exercises.hint: Am Montag is position 1. What must come next?
exercises.explanation: "Am Montag beginnt der Kurs." — Am Montag (P1, time) → beginnt (P2, verb) → der Kurs (P3, subject). After any first element, verb must be P2. "Am Montag der Kurs beginnt" puts the verb at P3 — incorrect in a main clause.
exercises.rule_hint: Time first → inversion. Am Montag beginnt der Kurs. Verb stays at P2.

lessons.title: Inversion — when the subject moves
lessons.content: When any element other than the subject starts the sentence, the subject and verb swap positions — this is called inversion. The verb stays in position 2. The subject jumps to position 3. The meaning does not change, only the emphasis.

exercises.question: What happens to the subject when a time expression starts a German sentence?
exercises.hint: Think about inversion
options: The subject moves to position 3, after the verb
The subject stays in position 1 before the time expression
The subject is not needed anymore
exercises.correct_answer: The subject moves to position 3, after the verb
exercises.explanation: When a time expression (or any element other than the subject) starts the sentence, the subject moves to position 3 — after the verb. This is called inversion. "Gestern habe ich..." — Gestern (P1) + habe (P2) + ich (P3). The verb stays fixed at P2; the subject adapts.
exercises.rule_hint: Inversion: non-subject first → verb P2 → subject P3. Verb never moves.

exercises.question: Which is the correct inverted version of "Ich gehe morgen zum Arzt"?
exercises.hint: Put morgen first — what must come immediately after it?
exercises.explanation: "Morgen gehe ich zum Arzt." — Morgen (P1, time) + gehe (P2, verb — immediately after the first element) + ich (P3, subject pushed back) + zum Arzt (place). "Morgen ich gehe" is wrong — verb must be P2.
exercises.rule_hint: Inverted: Morgen gehe ich... Time (P1) → Verb (P2) → Subject (P3).

exercises.question: Why would you use inverted word order in Goethe Schreiben?
exercises.hint: Think about emphasis and what it signals to the examiner
options: It is required — German always needs inversion
To emphasise a particular element and demonstrate word order mastery
Because German does not use subject-verb-object order
exercises.correct_answer: To emphasise a particular element and demonstrate word order mastery
exercises.explanation: Using inverted order is optional but signals to the Goethe examiner that you control German word order. "Morgen gehe ich zum Arzt" emphasises "tomorrow" and shows you know how German emphasis works. Using inversion occasionally in Schreiben raises your grammar mark.
exercises.rule_hint: Inversion = optional emphasis + grammar score. Use it at least once in Schreiben tasks.

lessons.title: Question word order — verb comes first or second
lessons.content: For yes/no questions (questions without a question word), the verb moves to position 1. The subject drops to position 2. There is nothing before the verb. W-questions (Wer, Was, Wann, Wo, Wie, Warum, Woher, Wohin) follow the same rule as inverted statements: the question word is position 1, the verb is position 2, the subject is position 3.

exercises.question: How do you form a yes/no question in German?
exercises.hint: Think about what comes first when there is no question word
exercises.explanation: In yes/no questions, the verb moves to position 1. "Haben Sie einen Termin?" — Haben (P1, verb) + Sie (P2, subject). There is no question word; the verb itself signals the question. Compare statements: "Sie haben einen Termin" vs question: "Haben Sie einen Termin?"
exercises.rule_hint: Yes/no question: Verb first. "Haben Sie...?" "Ist das...?" "Kommen Sie...?"

exercises.question: What is the correct question form of "Sie wohnen in Berlin"?
exercises.hint: W-word first, then verb, then subject
exercises.explanation: "Wo wohnen Sie?" — Wo (W-word, P1) + wohnen (verb, P2) + Sie (subject, P3). W-questions follow the same pattern as inverted statements: question word (P1) → verb (P2) → subject (P3).
exercises.rule_hint: W-question: W-word (P1) → Verb (P2) → Subject (P3). Wo wohnen Sie?

exercises.question: Which question asks "What is your profession?"
exercises.hint: Beruf = profession. Think about the W-word and verb order.
exercises.explanation: "Was sind Sie von Beruf?" — Was (W-word, P1) + sind (verb, P2) + Sie (subject, P3) + von Beruf (clarifying phrase). This is the standard Goethe Sprechen question about profession. The examiner asks this in every A1 speaking test.
exercises.rule_hint: "Was sind Sie von Beruf?" — standard Goethe profession question. Learn verbatim.

lessons.title: Modal verbs — conjugated verb in P2, infinitive at the end
lessons.content: German has six modal verbs: können (can), müssen (must), wollen (want), sollen (should), dürfen (may), möchten (would like). When you use a modal verb, the modal takes position 2 and the main verb goes to the very end as an infinitive.

exercises.question: Where does the infinitive go in a sentence with a modal verb?
exercises.hint: Modal in P2 — where does the action verb go?
options: At the very end of the clause
exercises.explanation: The infinitive goes to the very end of the clause. "Ich möchte einen Termin machen" — möchte (P2, modal) ... machen (end, infinitive). Everything between the modal and the infinitive: objects, time, place, negation. The infinitive always waits at the end.
exercises.rule_hint: Modal verbs: [modal P2] + [everything] + [infinitive END].

exercises.question: "Ich ___ morgen nicht ___ ." (I cannot come tomorrow.) Fill the gaps.
exercises.hint: kann in P2, kommen at the end — where does nicht go?
exercises.explanation: "kann morgen nicht kommen" — kann (modal, P2), morgen (time), nicht (negation), kommen (infinitive, end). TeKaMoLo applies in the middle: time before manner before place. nicht comes just before the infinitive it negates. Full sentence: "Ich kann morgen nicht kommen."
exercises.rule_hint: Cannot: kann [time] nicht [infinitive]. Ich kann morgen nicht kommen.

exercises.question: Which modal verb is most appropriate for polite requests in Goethe Schreiben?
exercises.hint: Think about register and politeness
exercises.explanation: "möchten" — would like to. möchten is the polite form for making requests in written and formal spoken German. "Ich möchte einen Termin machen" (I would like to make an appointment) sounds professional. "Ich will einen Termin" sounds demanding. Always use möchten in Schreiben requests.
exercises.rule_hint: Polite requests: möchten, not wollen. Ich möchte... is the Goethe standard.

lessons.title: Subordinate clauses — verb goes to the end
lessons.content: When you connect two clauses with a subordinating conjunction (weil, dass, wenn, obwohl, weil...), the verb in the second clause moves to the very end. This is called Verb-Letzt (verb-last). It is the most reliable way to show complex grammar in Goethe Schreiben.

exercises.question: In a subordinate clause with "weil", where does the verb go?
exercises.hint: weil is a subordinating conjunction
options: Position 2, same as a main clause
At the very end of the subordinate clause
It stays where it normally is
exercises.correct_answer: It stays where it normally is
exercises.explanation: After subordinating conjunctions (weil, dass, wenn, obwohl...), the verb moves to the very end of the clause. "Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten will." — "will" goes to the end. In the main clause "Ich lerne" is normal V2. The weil-clause has verb-last.
exercises.rule_hint: After weil/dass/wenn: verb goes to the END. "...weil ich arbeiten will."

exercises.question: Which sentence is grammatically correct?
exercises.hint: Check verb position in both clauses
options: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich will arbeiten in Deutschland.
Ich lerne Deutsch, weil in Deutschland ich arbeiten will.
Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten will.
Weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten will ich lerne Deutsch.
exercises.correct_answer: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten will.
exercises.explanation: "Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten will." — Main clause: V2 (lerne in P2). weil-clause: subject (ich) → middle (in Deutschland) → verb-end (arbeiten will). The modal "will" is at the very end after the infinitive "arbeiten".
exercises.rule_hint: Weil-clause: subject + middle + [infinitive + modal]. "...weil ich arbeiten will."

exercises.question: Why should you use "weil" sentences in Goethe Schreiben?
exercises.hint: Think about grammar scoring
options: Because examiners require weil in every sentence
Because it is shorter than two separate sentences
Because it demonstrates subordinate clause grammar and raises your score
Because weil is always more accurate than simple sentences
exercises.correct_answer: Because it demonstrates subordinate clause grammar and raises your score
exercises.explanation: Using weil-sentences demonstrates subordinate clause control — one of the grammar structures the Goethe examiner looks for in Schreiben. A sentence like "Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte" shows more grammatical range than two simple sentences. It directly improves your Schreiben score.
exercises.rule_hint: Weil-sentences = grammar demonstration. Use them for reasons in Schreiben.

lessons.title: TeKaMoLo — the order inside the sentence
lessons.content: German sentences often contain multiple adverbial elements. German has a rule — not always absolute but almost always correct — for their sequence: Temporal → Kausal → Modal → Lokal. Time → Cause/Reason → Manner → Place.

exercises.question: What is the correct TeKaMoLo order?
exercises.hint: Te-Ka-Mo-Lo — what do the letters stand for?
exercises.explanation: TeKaMoLo: Temporal (time) → Kausal (cause/reason) → Modal (manner/how) → Lokal (place/location). Time comes first, place comes last. "Ich fahre morgen mit dem Zug nach Berlin" — morgen (Te) + mit dem Zug (Mo) + nach Berlin (Lo).
exercises.rule_hint: TeKaMoLo: Time → Cause → Manner → Place. Time first, place last.

exercises.question: "Ich fahre ___ ___ nach Berlin." Which order is correct?
exercises.hint: Which comes first — manner or time?
exercises.explanation: "morgen mit dem Zug" — morgen = time (Te), mit dem Zug = manner (Mo), nach Berlin = place (Lo). Full sentence: "Ich fahre morgen mit dem Zug nach Berlin." Time before manner before place = TeKaMoLo.
exercises.rule_hint: Time before manner: morgen (Te) + mit dem Zug (Mo) + nach Berlin (Lo).

exercises.question: Which sentence demonstrates the most grammatical complexity for Goethe Schreiben?
exercises.hint: Look for the sentence that uses the most grammar rules correctly
options: Ich lerne jeden Tag Deutsch im Kurs.
Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
exercises.correct_answer: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
exercises.explanation: "Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte." — This uses: (1) main clause V2, (2) subordinating conjunction weil, (3) verb-last in the weil-clause, (4) modal verb möchten with infinitive at end. Four grammar features in one sentence. This is what earns the highest Schreiben marks.
exercises.rule_hint: Complex sentences with weil + modal demonstrate maximum grammar range for Goethe Schreiben.

title: Present TenseVerb Conjugation
description: In German, the verb changes its ending for every pronoun. This is called conjugation — and it is one of the most tested skills in the Goethe A1 exam. Many learners apply the English instinct — treating the verb as mostly unchanged — and write "er lerne" instead of "er lernt". This module fixes that — permanently.

lessons.title: What is present tense — and why does it matter?
